text:
The last page of a book is a small and specific thing. It holds the final sentence, and after that sentence, white space. Nothing more happens.

There is something people make of this. They slow down before it. They reread the paragraph before the last one. They put the book face-down on their chest and stare at the ceiling. This behavior is common enough that it has a name in some circles: the reluctant ending. But the page itself does not know any of this.

What the last page actually is: paper, ink, a page number in the corner if the designer kept it there, sometimes a colophon at the bottom naming the typeface or the printer. The text ends. The story ends. These are not the same ending, though people treat them as one.

The last page of a novel is different from the last page of a manual. The manual's last page is usually an index entry or a warranty disclaimer. Nobody lingers there. Nobody holds that page between their fingers and thinks about time. But the information it contains may be more practically useful than anything in the novel.

A book has a last page because it has a physical form. An ebook has a last screen, which can expand to include reader notes, similar titles, a star rating prompt. The platform keeps going even when the text stops. This changes the experience, though people argue about whether the change matters.

The last page does one thing reliably. It tells you the book is over. This sounds obvious. It is obvious. But there is value in the obvious: you know where you are, you know what has ended, you can set it down. The uncertainty is gone. You finished or you stopped, and either way the page is the same page.

Some last pages are famous. Some are forgotten the moment you close the cover. The page does not know the difference. It just sits there at the back, doing its job.
